elements.sent-2411-001: A famous conjecture of Chowla on the least primes in arithmetic progressions implies that the abscissa of convergence of the Weil representation zeta function for a procyclic group $G$ only depends on the set $S$ of primes dividing the order of $G$ and that it agrees with the abscissa of the Dedekind zeta function of $\mathbb{Z [p^{-1}\mid p \not\in S]$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Here we show that these consequences hold unconditionally for random procyclic groups in a suitable model.
elements.sent-2411-003: As a corollary, every real number $1 \leq \beta \leq 2$ is the Weil abscissa of some procyclic group.
elements.sent-2411-004: Dirichlet generating functions are a well-established tool in enumerative algebra, especially in asymptotic group theory; see for instance \cite{duSautoySegal, Voll1 .}
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, generating functions enumerating complex irreducible representations of infinite groups received considerable attention; see~\cite{AKOV,KiKl,LL and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In \cite{CCKV24 a generating function enumerating absolutely irreducible representations of a (profinite) group $G$ over all finite fields was defined that is reminiscent of the Hasse-Weil zeta function of an algebraic variety $V$; cf.~\cite{Weil .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: This Weil representation zeta function $\zeta_G^W(s)$ of a profinite group $G$ converges absolutely for $\Re(s)$ sufficiently large, if $G$ has ``uniformly bounded exponential representation growth'' (UBERG); see \cite[Cor.~2.3]{CCKV24 and \cite{KV for more on UBERG groups.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The abscissa of convergence $\alpha(G)$ of $\zeta^W_G(s)$ -- called Weil abscissa here -- is an intricate invariant.
elements.sent-2411-009: In \cite[Thm.~C]{CCKV24 the Weil abscissa of free pronilpotent and free prosoluable groups was determined.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Let $G$ be a finitely generated profinite abelian group.
elements.sent-2411-011: Then $G$ has UBERG and -- since absolutely irreducible representations of $G$ are one-dimensional -- the Weil representation zeta function is \begin{equation}\label{eq:defn_zeta} \zeta^W_G(s) := \exp \Bigl( \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}}\sum_{j=1}^\infty |\Hom(G,\bbF_{p^j}^\times)| \frac{p^{-sj}}{j}\Bigr)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{P}$ denotes the set of primes.
elements.sent-2411-012: The Weil abscissa is known for some abelian groups, e.g. $\alpha(\widehat{\bbZ ^r) = r+1$ and $\alpha(\bbZ_p) = 1$ for every prime $p$ \cite[Thm.~C]{CCKV24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The investigation of the Weil abscissa for $G = \bbZ_p^r$ in \cite{CCKV24 showed that the Weil abscissa can be related to questions about primes in arithmetic progressions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For every non-empty set $S$ of primes, define the procyclic groups $H_S = \prod_{p \in S \bbZ_p$ and $G_S = \prod_{p\in S C_p$ (here $C_p$ is cyclic of order $p$).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Here we investigate the Weil abscissa of $H_S$ and $G_S$ inspired by the following conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-017: Here $\alpha_D(S)$ denotes the abscissa of convergence of the partial Riemann zeta function $\zeta_S(s) = \prod_{p \in S (1-p^{-s )^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: We note that $\zeta_S$ agrees with the Dedekind zeta function of the ring $\bbZ_{S' = \bbZ[\frac{1 {p \mid p \not\in S]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Conjecture \ref{conjA is prompted by number theoretic considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-020: With a bit of elementary number theory one can show (see Lemma \ref{lem:basic-lemma below) \begin{equation}\frac{1}{L}(\alpha_D(S)+1) \leq \alpha(G_S) \leq \alpha(H_S) \leq \alpha_D(S)+1.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Linnik showed that such a constant exists.
elements.sent-2411-022: Currently $L=5$ is the best known value \cite{Xylouris .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Chowla conjectured that $L = 1+\epsilon$ is a Linnik constant for every $\epsilon > 0$; see \cite{Chowla1934 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, currently Chowla's conjecture seems to be out of reach and (as Chowla noticed) even the generalized Riemann hypothesis only gives $L = 2+\epsilon$ for every $\epsilon > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-027: It implies that the Weil abscissa of a procyclic group $G$ only depends by the set $S$ of primes dividing the order of $G$.. Another consequence is:.
elements.sent-2411-028: Every real number $1\leq \beta \leq 2$ is the abscissa of convergence of a procyclic group.
elements.sent-2411-029: The purpose of this paper is to prove Conjecture \ref{conjA almost surely for randomly chosen sets $S$ of primes.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This will enable us to deduce the validity of Conjecture \ref{conjVal .}

elements.sent-2411-036: It should be noted that $G_S$ and $H_S$ are the minimal and maximal procyclic groups with $S$ the set of primes dividing the order.
elements.sent-2411-037: This shows that for every ``random'' procyclic group $G$ such that the primes dividing the order of $G$ are distributed in the $\delta$-model, the Weil abscissa satisfies $\alpha(G) = 2- \delta$ almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-038: In other words, how ``small'' can a set $S$ with $\alpha(G_S) = 2$ be? Modifying the proof of a result of Mirsky \cite{Mirsky , we derive the following theorem.}

elements.sent-2411-042: Let $S \subseteq \mathcal{P}$ be a set of prime numbers.
elements.sent-2411-043: Throughout $S' = \mathcal{P \setminus S$ denotes the complement of $S$ in the set of all prime numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We write $\langle S \rangle$ to denote the set of all $S$-numbers.
elements.sent-2411-046: For $n \in \bbN$, we denote the largest $S$-number dividing $n$ by $n_S$.
elements.sent-2411-047: In particular, we have $n = n_S n_{S'}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: We write $\rad(n)$ to denote the radical of $n$ and we put $\rad_S(n) := \rad(n)_S$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The Dedekind zeta function of the ring $\bbZ_{S' = \mathbb{Z [{S' ^{-1 ]$ is the partial Riemann zeta function \begin{equation}\zeta_{S}(s) = \sum_{m \in \langle S \rangle} m^{-s} = \prod_{p \in S} (1-p^{-s})^{-1}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Recall that the abscissa of convergence of $\zeta_S$ is $\alpha_D(S)$; clearly $ \alpha_D(S) \leq 1$.

elements.sent-2411-052: If $S \neq \emptyset$, then the Weil abscissae satisfy \begin{equation}\frac{1}{L}(\alpha_D(S)+1) \leq \alpha(G_S) \leq \alpha(H_S) \leq \alpha_D(S)+1\end{equation} where $L$ denotes a Linnik constant.
elements.sent-2411-053: As $G_S$ is a quotient of $H_S$ the inequality $\alpha(G_S)\leq \alpha(H_S)$ is clear (cf.~\cite[Lemma 4.1]{CCKV24 ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $\Lambda$ denote the von Mangoldt function.
elements.sent-2411-057: We define $\Lambda'(n) = \frac{\Lambda(n) {\log(n)}$ for $n \geq 2$ and we put $\Lambda'(1) = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: For all real $s > \alpha_D(S)+1$ we have \begin{equation}\log \zeta^{W}_{G_S}(s) = \sum_{n \geq 2} (n-1)_S \ \Lambda'(n)n^{-s} \leq \sum_{n\geq 2} (n-1)_S \ n^{-s} \leq \sum_{m\geq 1} m_S \ m^{-s} = \sum_{k \in \langle S\rangle, \ell \in \langle S' \rangle} k (k\ell)^{-s} = \zeta_{S}(s-1) \zeta_{S'}(s).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-062: Define $\mu_2(m) = 1$ if $m$ is square-free and $\mu_2(m) =0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-063: The Dirichlet series \begin{equation}\sum_{m \in \bbN_{S}} \mu_2(m) m^{-s} = \prod_{p \in S} (1+p^{-s}) = \frac{\zeta_{S}(s)}{\zeta_{S}(2s)}\end{equation} has the same absicssa of convergence as $\zeta_{S}$.

elements.sent-2411-065: Every prime $p$ equals $p(m)$ for at most $d(p-1)$ different numbers $m$, where $d$ denotes the divisor function.
elements.sent-2411-067: The well-known divisor bound asserts that $d(p-1) = O_\epsilon(p^\epsilon)$ and this allows to conclude \begin{equation}\log(\zeta^{W}_{G_S})(s) \geq \sum_{p} \rad_S(p-1) p^{-s} \geq \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) \rad_S(p(m)-1)d(p(m))^{-1} p(m)^{-s} \gg \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) m p(m)^{-s-\epsilon} \gg \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) m^{1-L(s+\epsilon)}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\log(\zeta^{W}_{G_S})(s) \geq \sum_{p} \rad_S(p-1) p^{-s} \geq \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) \rad_S(p(m)-1)d(p(m))^{-1} p(m)^{-s} \gg \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) m p(m)^{-s-\epsilon} \gg \sum_{m \in \langle S\rangle} \mu_2(m) m^{1-L(s+\epsilon)}.\end{equation}

elements.section-2411-003: A variation of a result of Mirsky
elements.sent-2411-070: Mirsky \cite{Mirsky proved that the set of prime numbers $p$ such that $p-1$ is $k$-free has a positive natural density $\prod_{p \bigl(1- \frac{1 {(p-1)p^{k-1 }\bigr)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Here we need a slight variation of his result.
elements.sent-2411-073: We say that a natural number $n$ is $f$-bounded, if \begin{equation}\nu_p(n) \leq f(n)\end{equation} for all primes $p$.
elements.sent-2411-075: If $f$ is the constant function with value $k-1$, then $f$-bounded numbers are exactly the $k$-free numbers.
elements.sent-2411-076: Recall that a set $S$ is primes is thin, if $\alpha_D(S) < 1$.
elements.sent-2411-077: A set of primes is thick, if its complement is thin.
elements.sent-2411-078: A function $f\colon \mathcal{P \to \bbN_0$ is thick, if its support is thick; i.e., $f^{-1 (0)$ is thin.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Following closely along the lines of Mirsky's approach, one obtains: \begin{thm Let $f\colon \mathcal{P \to \mathbb{N _0$ be thick and let $A > 0$ be arbitrary.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Let $U_f(x)$ denote the set of primes $p \leq x$ such that $p-1$ is $f$-bounded.
elements.sent-2411-081: Then \begin{equation}|U_f(x)| = \prod_{p} \left(1- \frac{1}{(p-1)p^{f(p)}}\right) Li(x) + O\Bigl(\frac{x}{\log^A(x)}\Bigr)\end{equation} as $x$ tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-083: Since Mirsky omitted the proof of his theorem in \cite{Mirsky , we decided to include a proof.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $p$ be a prime, we write $p^{(f) := p^{f(p)}$ and extend this multiplicatively to all natural numbers, i.e. for $n = \prod_{i=1 ^r p_i^{e_i}$ we have $n^{(f) = \prod_{i=1 ^r p_i^{f(p_i)e_i}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-122: To bound $\Sigma_2$ we use the observation $\pi(t,a,1) \leq \frac{t {a}$ and deduce \begin{equation}\Sigma_2 \leq \sum_{t^\theta< a \leq t}a^\delta \frac{t^2}{a^2} = t^2 \sum_{t^\theta< a \leq t} a^{\delta- 2} \ll t^2\int_{t^\theta}^tx^{\delta-2} dx \ll t^{2+(\delta-1)\theta} \ll \frac{t^2}{\log^{A}(t)},\end{equation} since $\delta < 1$.}

elements.sent-2411-130: The expectation of $I_n$ is the probability that $n$ is an $S$-number, i.e. that all prime factors of $n$ belong to $S$: \begin{equation}\bfE(I_n) = \prod_{p \mid n}\frac{p^{1-\delta}-1}{p-1} = \rad(n)^{-\delta} \prod_{p\mid n} \frac{p-p^{\delta}}{p-1} \leq \rad(n)^{-\delta} .\end{equation}.
